summary: I am Sandy Waugh Bradshaw, originally from Climax, North Carolina, and I worked as a flight attendant for United Airlines. By the time of the September 11 attacks, I had been flying for eleven years but was only doing two trips a month to spend more time with my family, including my two toddlers, teenage stepdaughter, and my husband who is a pilot for US Airways. On that tragic day, I was aboard United Airlines Flight 93 and took immediate action by calling United Airlines to report the hijacking and describing the terrorists. I also managed to call my husband at our home in Greensboro, North Carolina, to update him on the situation, including how we were boiling water to defend ourselves. It was a terrifying experience, but I did my best to help protect the passengers and crew.

The United States National Park Service maintains the official memorial and information about Flight 93 and its crew, including myself, Sandy Waugh Bradshaw. They provide resources and updates about the events of September 11, 2001, and honor the bravery of those involved. Their website and app help visitors explore the Flight 93 National Memorial and learn about the history and heroism connected to that day. I appreciate how the National Park Service preserves this important part of our nation's history.

I worked as a flight attendant for United Airlines for over a decade, and on September 11, 2001, I was aboard United Airlines Flight 93. During the hijacking, I called United Airlines to report the emergency and describe the terrorists, trying to get help for everyone on board. United Airlines was my employer, and I felt a strong responsibility to communicate what was happening to them in hopes of coordinating a response. My experience with United Airlines shaped much of my career and was central to the events of that tragic day.

My husband is a pilot for US Airways, and during the events of September 11, 2001, I was able to call him from United Airlines Flight 93 to update him on the hijacking situation. Being connected to US Airways through my husband gave me a personal link to the aviation community beyond my own role at United Airlines. We lived together in Greensboro, North Carolina, and his support was crucial during that terrifying time. US Airways was part of my family's life, even though I flew for a different airline.

Greensboro, North Carolina, is where my family and I lived during the time I worked as a flight attendant for United Airlines. It was from our home in Greensboro that I called my husband during the hijacking on September 11, 2001, to let him know what was happening on Flight 93. Greensboro was the place I returned to between flights, where my two toddlers, teenage stepdaughter, and husband awaited me. It was my safe haven amid the chaos of that day.

I grew up in Climax, North Carolina, which is where my roots are and where my love for people and travel began. Being from Climax, I always dreamed of becoming a flight attendant, and eventually, I achieved that dream with United Airlines. My background from this small town shaped who I am and gave me the foundation to face the challenges I encountered, including those on September 11, 2001. Climax will always be a special part of my story.

The September 11 attacks were a defining moment in my life and career as a flight attendant on United Airlines Flight 93. On that day, terrorists hijacked our plane, and I was involved in reporting the situation to United Airlines and communicating with my husband at home in Greensboro, North Carolina. We tried to defend ourselves by boiling water to throw on the hijackers, and I witnessed passengers moving toward first class to confront the threat. The events of that day tested all of us, and I am proud of the courage shown by everyone on board.

I was a flight attendant on United Airlines Flight 93 on September 11, 2001, when the plane was hijacked by terrorists. During the ordeal, I called United Airlines to report the emergency and described the hijackers, and I also called my husband at our home in Greensboro, North Carolina, to keep him informed. We were boiling water to defend ourselves, and passengers were moving toward first class to try to regain control of the plane. Flight 93 was a terrifying and heroic experience that I will never forget.
